The surgeon examines the vascularity of the tumor, identifying an artery that branches posteriorly and enters the tumor, which will need to be excised. The anterior branch of the right renal artery is also visible, bifurcating into posterior and anterior branches.

The surgeon identifies a small arterial branch and a larger branch, noting that the larger branch supplies the tumor directly. The tumor, approximately four and a half centimeters in size, is located centrally within the kidney, replacing the central part of the organ.

The surgical team prepares for a transperitoneal approach using a robotic system with two assistant ports. They reflect the colon and small bowel medially, retract the ureter and gonadal vein laterally, and follow the psoas muscle to the main renal hilum. Vessel loops are placed around the main renal artery and vein, and the kidney is circumferentially mobilized. The ureter is freed from the kidney, which is rotated 180 degrees to bring the posterior surface anterior. An intraoperative ultrasound is performed to score the tumor, followed by clamping of the main renal artery and vein. Dissection is carried out to locate and address the posterior branch entering the tumor.

The surgeon assesses the kidney, noting areas of pink tissue and less perfused regions. A stitch is placed with caution to avoid disrupting underlying blood vessels, while maintaining hemostasis. A wet clip is removed, and the urethral catheter is adjusted by pulling it down five centimeters.

The surgeon closes off the collecting system using sutures, noting the importance of following the needle's curve in the vascularized kidney to prevent excessive bleeding. The surgeon points out the thrombus adjacent to the tumor, which is in contact with the central sinus. The intact capsule and collecting system are visible. The parenchyma loss is limited to the area directly above the tumor, with minimal devascularization observed during extraction.

The surgeon uses absorbable clips to secure the area after scoring the tumor with ultrasound. The scoring is deepened circumferentially to free up the area, and the dissection proceeds from the hilum towards the kidney, following the main renal artery. Wet clips are strategically placed to minimize suturing, with stitches only applied in specific areas. The line of demarcation on the kidney is observed to have shrunk, indicating improved perfusion.

The surgeon carefully pulls the stitch vertically to avoid tearing, applies a wet clip, and cinches it down tightly to ensure it does not cut through the tissue. The kidney is flipped over to check hemostasis, revealing a pink and healthy appearance on the other side. The surgeon uses suction irrigation to clean the area and recommends the use of striker suction for effective disposal.

The surgeon evaluates the vascularity of the kidney, noting the lower pole appears healthy while some posterior areas show a bluish hue. The anterior section looks satisfactory. The surgeon prepares to place two nephropexy stitches to prevent kidney torquing and potential infarction, ensuring a stable fixation.
